Graded Novelty Encoding Task - pilot analysis

The paradigm

-Familiarization phase Participants see 5 stimuli repeated 8 times each. Stimuli consist of a 3x3 grid, with 5 random shapes (circle,triangle,square) placed randomly (any 9 cells) with random color (red,blue,yellow,green,orange,purple).

-Study phase Participants see the 5 familiar stimuli 5 times each (25) and 50 novel stimuli. They have to judge if the current stimulus is one of the familiars. The novels differ to a differing degree from the familiars: there is 5x2 (two for each familiar) that is wholly different (diffarr). 5x2 that has the same placement of random shapes (sarr), 5x2 same arrangement and shapes (sarrsha), 5x2 same arrangement and colors (sarrcol) 5x2 same arrangement, shapes and colors (colors are randomized, thus, these are not the same as the familiars - sarrshacol).

-Test phase Participants see the novels from the study phase and 50 new stimuli (sarr - 10, sarrsha - 10, etc.). Participants have to signal if they have already seen the current stimulus or it is wholly new. They had the option to signal “maybe”, but were asked to do this in moderation (maybe resp <= 20). These responses have been added to correct responses.

Participants

The online task has been started a total of 152 times.

We excluded a total of 11 runs due to irregular data leaving 141 in the analysis.

We excluded some (6) runs as they missed more than 66 of 175 trials.

We also excluded 27 runs in which the participant produced less than 0.3 corrected recognition rate for familiars in the study phase.

We also excluded 2 participants as they overused “maybe” responses in the test phase (more than 25).

We also excluded 1 run(s) as only the first run is kept in the analysis from one participant.

This left a total of 105 participants.

Maybe responses [mean(±sd)] : 12.3301887 ± 9.0970657

The sample had a mean(±sd) age of 24.5576923 ± 6.7519532.

The sample contained 88 women.

The histogram below shows the number of participants completing the task in a given hour of the day.

## corrplot 0.90 loaded

Study phase:

During the study phase, the participants see familiar and novel items.

The participants produced a mean (±sd) recognition rate of 0.6586667 ± 0.1666619 for familiar pictures.

The participants produced a mean (±sd) corrected recognition rate of 0.3973333 ± 0.2131211 for familiar pictures.

Uncorrected: The sample performed above chance levels, as a one sample t-test showed (t(209)=13.7961814, p = 1.6389635^{-31}, Cohen’s d = 0.952027, 95% CI:0.6649518 - 1.2391021).

Corrected: The sample performed above chance levels, as a one sample t-test showed (t(209)=27.0170661, p = 2.0413423^{-70}, Cohen’s d = 1.8643547, 95% CI:1.5384899 - 2.1902195).

We use an ANOVA to see if there is a difference in correct rejection for the different novels.

##  contrast  estimate    SE  df t.ratio p.value

##  quartic      4.400 1.096 104   4.014  0.0001

Test phase:

This phase tests the learning that has occurred during the study phase. If the participants learned the novels in the study phase, their responses should differ for the stimuli that they have already seen during the study phase (familiars) and those that they see for the first time during the test phase (novels). We also let the participants to choose “maybe” by pressing the down arrow, but asked them not to overuse this response (maybe resp <= 50). We added these responses as correct responses to both stimulus categories.

Maybe responses (MEAN ± SD): 12.3142857 ± 9.1392163.

## 
## Attaching package: 'rstatix'
## The following object is masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter
## [1] "A t-test to compare the number of old responses btwn old and new stim"

Results of the paired t-test: (t(1049)=5.5959762, p = 2.7991796^{-8}, Cohen’s d = 0.1544533, 95% CI:0.1000037 - 0.2089029).

Shapiro-Wilk is significant (p = 9.4885676^{-14}).

We used a Wilcoxon sgined-rank test. Z = -5.821533, p = 2.9155149^{-9}

ANOVA - Corrected Recog

##  quartic    -1.2190 1.453 104  -0.839  0.4034

ANOVA (factor: TYPE (5 lvls)): (F[3.7812883,393.2539784] = 9.4474138, p = 4.9846388^{-7}, n2G = 0.0641624).

Mixed models

The metrics

-DTF - Difference To corresponding Familiar picture All pictures are compared to a familiar. The number of differences between the three vectors of the pictures yields a difference score.

-DTAF - mean Difference To All Familiars We calculate the difference from all prototypes and take the mean for each picture in the set.

-DTN - mean Difference To all other Novels in the same category We calculate the differences between each pair of pictures and then take the mean of these differences (19 comparisons per stim).

Study phase

With these models i tried to assess which difference index explains familiar v novel distinction in study phase the best. As far as i understand, the model with the smallest AIC fits best to our data, which means that the mean difference from the corresponding familiar worked best here. The models all show, that the more different pictures are categorized with the highest efficacy.

## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]

Test phase

Models #1 - without random slope term

Logistic mixed regression for the test phase. Does difference from prototypes or familiarity/novelty have an effect on correct categorization in the test phase?

These models use trial quality (familiar / novel) as a fixed and as a random effect.

rt_resp is recognition test response, 0 stands for ‘old’ and 1 stands for ‘new’.

Models #2 - with random slope (trial quality)

Logistic mixed regression for the test phase. Does difference from prototypes or familiarity/novelty have an effect on correct categorization in the test phase?

These models use trial quality (familiar / novel) as a fixed effect only.

rt_resp is recognition test response, 0 stands for ‘old’ and 1 stands for ‘new’.
